text: Open nephro-ureterectomy remains a standard for treatment of upper tract transitional cell carcinoma. Laparoscopic nephro-ureterectomy was performed in an effort to decrease patient morbidity However, adhering to the oncologic principle of removal of a bladder cuff was found to be very difficult using the laparoscopic approach.

Robotic nephro-uterectomy provides the morbidity advantage of a minimally invasive approach, but improves upon the laparoscopic technique by allowing complete removal of the bladder cuff and sutured closure of the bladder. It also provides complete exposure of the entire retroperitoneum. The current robotic platform, the DaVinci XI, provides complete access to the abdomen and pelvis using four robotic arms and a single docking approach. The improved access also facilitates retroperitoneal lymph node dissection of the perihilar lymph nodes.

To demonstrate our technique of robotic nephro-ureterectomy, we present the case of a seventy-two-year-old female who presented with gross hematuria and on CT scan was found to have a one point seven centimeter nodule in the lower pole calyx. Ureteroscopic biopsy revealed high-grade transitional cell carcinoma and metastatic evaluation with chest imaging was negative. The patient had normal renal function.

Given these findings, our plan was for left robotic nephro-ureterectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection using the da Vinci XI. The procedure is performed with the patient in a modified flank position, and a linear four-port configuration is used with the ports angled medially to allow complete access to the pelvis. The robot is docked midway between the kidney and bladder over the iliac crest.

The surgeon begins the procedure by mobilizing the colon and dissecting the mesocolon off Gerota's fascia. The ureter is identified and clipped at the mid-ureter to prevent tumor cell migration into the bladder. The ureter is dissected down to the bladder before mobilizing the kidney, using the fourth robotic arm to provide traction and improve exposure in the pelvis.

The surgeon dissects the ureter into the pelvis until reaching the detrusor fibers, indicated by the ureter widening as it enters the bladder. The bladder mucosa is incised, and a barbed suture is anchored to the bladder opening. The bladder cuff is released with scissors and then sutured to prevent retraction of the bladder opening, ensuring the cuff is not completely transected before suturing.

The surgeon identifies the ureteral orifice and completely releases the bladder cuff. Following the release, the cystotomy is closed in two layers using a barbed suture. The bladder is then filled to confirm a watertight closure.

The surgeon focuses on the kidney, isolating the renal vein and renal artery. The renal artery and vein are divided using an endoscopic stapler. The dissection continues to develop the plane between the upper pole of the kidney and the adrenal gland, ensuring the adrenal gland remains in situ. The posterior and lateral attachments of the kidney are divided, releasing the kidney.

The surgeon performs a retroperitoneal lymph node dissection, isolating the periaortic lymphatics starting at the renal hilum and moving inferiorly. Polymer clips are used to control the lymphatic tissue surrounding the aorta, and the inferior extent of the lymphatic packet is secured with additional clips. The lymphatic packet is then placed into an endoscopic retrieval bag.

Operative time was two point five hours and the patient was discharged on post-operative day three Final pathology revealed high-grade transitional cell carcinoma with invasion into the renal parenchyma with negative margins and negative lymph nodes. Pathologic stage was T-three and zero. Postoperatively, the patient's EGFR fell to the expected level, and there was no complication. At follow-up of twenty-four months, there was no evidence of recurrence.

Our experience at Swedish Medical Center with nephro-ureterectomy includes thirty-five robotic procedures, with the majority being performed using the da Vinci XI. Mean operative time for the group was just over four hours, with minimal blood loss. The mean length of stay was two point seven days, and the average number of lymph nodes removed was eleven. In conclusion, robotic nephrourectomy offers the advantage of reduced morbidity compared to the open approach, but with superior reach and reconstructive capabilities compared to pure laparoscopy. This allows the complete removal of the bladder cuff and performance of the retroperitoneal lymph node dissection in keeping with the oncologic principles established by open nephrourectomy.
